CSE5313CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 25)

CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 25)

Polynomial Evaluation

Problem formulation:

  • We have KKK datasets X1,X2,…,XKX_1,X_2,\ldots,X_KX1​,X2​,…,XK​.
  • Want to compute some polynomial function fff of degree ddd on each dataset.
    • Want f(X1),f(X2),…,f(XK)f(X_1),f(X_2),\ldots,f(X_K)f(X1​),f(X2​),…,f(XK​).
  • Examples:
    • X1,X2,…,XKX_1,X_2,\ldots,X_KX1​,X2​,…,XK​ are points in FM×M\mathbb{F}^{M\times M}FM×M, and f(X)=X8+3X2+1f(X)=X^8+3X^2+1f(X)=X8+3X2+1.
    • Xk=(Xk(1),Xk(2))X_k=(X_k^{(1)},X_k^{(2)})Xk​=(Xk(1)​,Xk(2)​), both in FM×M\mathbb{F}^{M\times M}FM×M, and f(X)=Xk(1)Xk(2)f(X)=X_k^{(1)}X_k^{(2)}f(X)=Xk(1)​Xk(2)​.
    • Gradient computation.

PPP worker nodes:

  • Some are stragglers, i.e., not responsive.
  • Some are adversaries, i.e., return erroneous results.
  • Privacy: We do not want to expose datasets to worker nodes.

Lagrange Coded Computing

Let ℓ(z)\ell(z)ℓ(z) be a polynomial whose evaluations at ω1,…,ωK\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_{K}ω1​,…,ωK​ are X1,…,XKX_1,\ldots,X_KX1​,…,XK​.

  • That is, ℓ(ωi)=Xi\ell(\omega_i)=X_iℓ(ωi​)=Xi​ for every ωi∈F,i∈[K]\omega_i\in \mathbb{F}, i\in [K]ωi​∈F,i∈[K].

Some example constructions:

Given X1,…,XKX_1,\ldots,X_KX1​,…,XK​ with corresponding ω1,…,ωK\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_Kω1​,…,ωK​

  • ℓ(z)=∑i=1KXiℓi(z)\ell(z)=\sum_{i=1}^K X_i\ell_i(z)ℓ(z)=∑i=1K​Xi​ℓi​(z), where ℓi(z)=∏j∈[K],j≠iz−ωjωi−ωj={0if j≠i1if j=i\ell_i(z)=\prod_{j\in[K],j\neq i} \frac{z-\omega_j}{\omega_i-\omega_j}=\begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j\neq i \\ 1 & \text{if } j=i \end{cases}ℓi​(z)=∏j∈[K],j=i​ωi​−ωj​z−ωj​​={01​if j=iif j=i​.

Then every f(Xi)=f(ℓ(ωi))f(X_i)=f(\ell(\omega_i))f(Xi​)=f(ℓ(ωi​)) is an evaluation of polynomial f∘ℓ(z)f\circ \ell(z)f∘ℓ(z) at ωi\omega_iωi​.

If the master obtains the composition h=f∘ℓh=f\circ \ellh=f∘ℓ, it can obtain every f(Xi)=h(ωi)f(X_i)=h(\omega_i)f(Xi​)=h(ωi​).

Goal: The master wished to obtain the polynomial h(z)=f(ℓ(z))h(z)=f(\ell(z))h(z)=f(ℓ(z)).

Intuition:

  • Encoding is performed by evaluating ℓ(z)\ell(z)ℓ(z) at α1,…,αP∈F\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_P\in \mathbb{F}α1​,…,αP​∈F, and P>KP>KP>K for redundancy.
  • Nodes apply fff on an evaluation of ℓ\ellℓ and obtain an evaluation of hhh.
  • The master receives some potentially noisy evaluations, and finds hhh.
  • The master evaluates hhh at ω1,…,ωK\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_Kω1​,…,ωK​ to obtain f(X1),…,f(XK)f(X_1),\ldots,f(X_K)f(X1​),…,f(XK​).

Encoding for Lagrange coded computing

Need polynomial ℓ(z)\ell(z)ℓ(z) such that:

  • Xk=ℓ(ωk)X_k=\ell(\omega_k)Xk​=ℓ(ωk​) for every k∈[K]k\in [K]k∈[K].

Having obtained such ℓ\ellℓ we let X~i=ℓ(αi)\tilde{X}_i=\ell(\alpha_i)X~i​=ℓ(αi​) for every i∈[P]i\in [P]i∈[P].

spanX~1,X~2,…,X~P=spanℓ1(x),ℓ2(x),…,ℓP(x)span{\tilde{X}_1,\tilde{X}_2,\ldots,\tilde{X}_P}=span{\ell_1(x),\ell_2(x),\ldots,\ell_P(x)}spanX~1​,X~2​,…,X~P​=spanℓ1​(x),ℓ2​(x),…,ℓP​(x).

Want Xk=ℓ(ωk)X_k=\ell(\omega_k)Xk​=ℓ(ωk​) for every k∈[K]k\in [K]k∈[K].

Tool: Lagrange interpolation.

  • ℓk(z)=∏i≠kz−ωjωk−ωj\ell_k(z)=\prod_{i\neq k} \frac{z-\omega_j}{\omega_k-\omega_j}ℓk​(z)=∏i=k​ωk​−ωj​z−ωj​​.
  • ℓk(ωk)=1\ell_k(\omega_k)=1ℓk​(ωk​)=1 and ℓk(ωk)=0\ell_k(\omega_k)=0ℓk​(ωk​)=0 for every j≠kj\neq kj=k.
  • deg⁡ℓk(z)=K−1\deg \ell_k(z)=K-1degℓk​(z)=K−1.

Let ℓ(z)=∑k=1KXkℓk(z)\ell(z)=\sum_{k=1}^K X_k\ell_k(z)ℓ(z)=∑k=1K​Xk​ℓk​(z).

  • deg⁡ℓ≤K−1\deg \ell\leq K-1degℓ≤K−1.
  • ℓ(ωk)=Xk\ell(\omega_k)=X_kℓ(ωk​)=Xk​ for every k∈[K]k\in [K]k∈[K].

Let X~i=ℓ(αi)=∑k=1KXkℓk(αi)\tilde{X}_i=\ell(\alpha_i)=\sum_{k=1}^K X_k\ell_k(\alpha_i)X~i​=ℓ(αi​)=∑k=1K​Xk​ℓk​(αi​).

Every X~i\tilde{X}_iX~i​ is a linear combination of X1,…,XKX_1,\ldots,X_KX1​,…,XK​.

(X~1,X~2,…,X~P)=(X1,…,XK)⋅G=(X1,…,XK)[ℓ1(α1)ℓ1(α2)⋯ℓ1(αP)ℓ2(α1)ℓ2(α2)⋯ℓ2(αP)⋮⋮⋱⋮ℓK(α1)ℓK(α2)⋯ℓK(αP)](\tilde{X}_1,\tilde{X}_2,\ldots,\tilde{X}_P)=(X_1,\ldots,X_K)\cdot G=(X_1,\ldots,X_K)\begin{bmatrix} \ell_1(\alpha_1) & \ell_1(\alpha_2) & \cdots & \ell_1(\alpha_P) \\ \ell_2(\alpha_1) & \ell_2(\alpha_2) & \cdots & \ell_2(\alpha_P) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \ell_K(\alpha_1) & \ell_K(\alpha_2) & \cdots & \ell_K(\alpha_P) \end{bmatrix}(X~1​,X~2​,…,X~P​)=(X1​,…,XK​)⋅G=(X1​,…,XK​)​ℓ1​(α1​)ℓ2​(α1​)⋮ℓK​(α1​)​ℓ1​(α2​)ℓ2​(α2​)⋮ℓK​(α2​)​⋯⋯⋱⋯​ℓ1​(αP​)ℓ2​(αP​)⋮ℓK​(αP​)​​

This GGG is called a Lagrange matrix with respect to

  • ω1,…,ωK\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_Kω1​,…,ωK​. (interpolation points, rows)
  • α1,…,αP\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_Pα1​,…,αP​. (evaluation points, columns)

Basically, a modification of Reed-Solomon code.

Decoding for Lagrange coded computing

Say the system has SSS stragglers (erasures) and AAA adversaries (errors).

The master receives P−SP-SP−S computation results f(X~i1),…,f(X~iP−S)f(\tilde{X}_{i_1}),\ldots,f(\tilde{X}_{i_{P-S}})f(X~i1​​),…,f(X~iP−S​​).

  • By design, therese are evaluations of h:h(ai1)=f(ℓ(ai1)),…,h(aiP−S)=f(ℓ(aiP−S))h: h(a_{i_1})=f(\ell(a_{i_1})),\ldots,h(a_{i_{P-S}})=f(\ell(a_{i_{P-S}}))h:h(ai1​​)=f(ℓ(ai1​​)),…,h(aiP−S​​)=f(ℓ(aiP−S​​))
  • A evaluation are noisy
  • deg⁡h=deg⁡f⋅deg⁡ℓ=(K−1)deg⁡f\deg h=\deg f\cdot \deg \ell=(K-1)\deg fdegh=degf⋅degℓ=(K−1)degf.

Which process enables to interpolate a polynomial from noisy evaluations?

Ree-Solomon (RS) decoding.

Fact: Reed-Solomon decoding succeeds if and only if the number of erasures + 2 ×\times× the number of errors ≤d−1\leq d-1≤d−1.

Imagine hhh as the “message” in Reed-Solomon code. [P,(K−1)deg⁡f+1,P−(K−1)deg⁡f]q[P,(K-1)\deg f +1,P-(K-1)\deg f]_q[P,(K−1)degf+1,P−(K−1)degf]q​.

  • Interpolating hhh is possible if and only if S+2A≤(K−1)deg⁡f−1S+2A\leq (K-1)\deg f-1S+2A≤(K−1)degf−1.

Once the master interpolates hhh.

  • The evaluations h(ωi)=f(ℓ(ωi))=f(Xi)h(\omega_i)=f(\ell(\omega_i))=f(X_i)h(ωi​)=f(ℓ(ωi​))=f(Xi​) provides the interpolation results.

Theorem of Lagrange coded computing

Lagrange coded computing enables to compute {f(Xi)}i=1K\{f(X_i)\}_{i=1}^K{f(Xi​)}i=1K​ for any fff at the presence of at most SSS stragglers and at most AAA adversaries if

(K−1)deg⁡f+S+2A+1≤P(K-1)\deg f+S+2A+1\leq P(K−1)degf+S+2A+1≤P

Interpolation of result does not depend on PPP (number of worker nodes).

Privacy for Lagrange coded computing

Currently any size-KKK group of colluding nodes reveals the entire dataset.

Q: Can an individual node iii learn anything about XiX_iXi​?

A: Yes, since X~i\tilde{X}_iX~i​ is a linear combination of X1,…,XKX_1,\ldots,X_KX1​,…,XK​ (partial knowledge, a linear combination of private data).

Can we provide perfect privacy given that at most TTT nodes collude?

  • That is, I(X:X~i)=0I(X:\tilde{X}_i)=0I(X:X~i​)=0 for every T⊆[P]\mathcal{T}\subseteq [P]T⊆[P] of size at most TTT, where
    • X=(X1,…,XK)X=(X_1,\ldots,X_K)X=(X1​,…,XK​), and
    • X~T=(X~i1,…,X~i∣T∣)\tilde{X}_\mathcal{T}=(\tilde{X}_{i_1},\ldots,\tilde{X}_{i_{|\mathcal{T}|}})X~T​=(X~i1​​,…,X~i∣T∣​​).

Solution: Slight change of encoding in LLC.

This only applied to F=Fq\mathbb{F}=\mathbb{F}_qF=Fq​ (no perfect privacy over R,C\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}R,C. No uniform distribution can be defined).

The master chooses

  • TTT keys ZK+1,…,ZK+TZ_{K+1},\ldots,Z_{K+T}ZK+1​,…,ZK+T​ uniformly at random (∣Zi∣=∣Xi∣|Z_i|=|X_i|∣Zi​∣=∣Xi​∣ for all iii)
  • Interpolation points ω1,…,ωK+T\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_{K+T}ω1​,…,ωK+T​.

Find the Lagrange polynomial ℓ(z)\ell(z)ℓ(z) such that

  • ℓ(wi)=Xi\ell(w_i)=X_iℓ(wi​)=Xi​ for i∈[K]i\in [K]i∈[K]
  • ℓ(wK+j)=Zj\ell(w_{K+j})=Z_jℓ(wK+j​)=Zj​ for j∈[T]j\in [T]j∈[T].

Lagrange interpolation:

ℓ(z)=∑i=1KXiℓi(z)+∑j=1TXK+jellK+j(z)\ell(z)=\sum_{i=1}^{K} X_i\ell_i(z)+\sum_{j=1}^{T} X_{K+j}ell_{K+j}(z)ℓ(z)=i=1∑K​Xi​ℓi​(z)+j=1∑T​XK+j​ellK+j​(z) (X~1,…,X~P)=(X1,…,XK,Z1,…,ZT)⋅G(\tilde{X}_1,\ldots,\tilde{X}_P)=(X_1,\ldots,X_K,Z_1,\ldots,Z_T)\cdot G(X~1​,…,X~P​)=(X1​,…,XK​,Z1​,…,ZT​)⋅G

where

G=[ℓ1(α1)ℓ1(α2)⋯ℓ1(αP)ℓ2(α1)ℓ2(α2)⋯ℓ2(αP)⋮⋮⋱⋮ℓK(α1)ℓK(α2)⋯ℓK(αP)⋮⋮⋱⋮ℓK+T(α1)ℓK+T(α2)⋯ℓK+T(αP)]G=\begin{bmatrix} \ell_1(\alpha_1) & \ell_1(\alpha_2) & \cdots & \ell_1(\alpha_P) \\ \ell_2(\alpha_1) & \ell_2(\alpha_2) & \cdots & \ell_2(\alpha_P) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \ell_K(\alpha_1) & \ell_K(\alpha_2) & \cdots & \ell_K(\alpha_P) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \ell_{K+T}(\alpha_1) & \ell_{K+T}(\alpha_2) & \cdots & \ell_{K+T}(\alpha_P) \end{bmatrix}G=​ℓ1​(α1​)ℓ2​(α1​)⋮ℓK​(α1​)⋮ℓK+T​(α1​)​ℓ1​(α2​)ℓ2​(α2​)⋮ℓK​(α2​)⋮ℓK+T​(α2​)​⋯⋯⋱⋯⋱⋯​ℓ1​(αP​)ℓ2​(αP​)⋮ℓK​(αP​)⋮ℓK+T​(αP​)​​

For analysis, we denote G=[GtopGbot]G=\begin{bmatrix}G^{top}\\G^{bot}\end{bmatrix}G=[GtopGbot​], where Gtop∈FK×PG^{top}\in \mathbb{F}^{K\times P}Gtop∈FK×P and Gbot∈FT×PG^{bot}\in \mathbb{F}^{T\times P}Gbot∈FT×P.

The proof for privacy is the almost the same as ramp scheme.

Proof

We have (X~1,…X~P)=(X1,…,XK)⋅Gtop+(Z1,…,ZT)⋅Gbot(\tilde{X}_1,\ldots \tilde{X}_P)=(X_1,\ldots,X_K)\cdot G^{top}+(Z_1,\ldots,Z_T)\cdot G^{bot}(X~1​,…X~P​)=(X1​,…,XK​)⋅Gtop+(Z1​,…,ZT​)⋅Gbot.

Without loss of generality, T=[T]\mathcal{T}=[T]T=[T] is the colluding set.

T\mathcal{T}T hold (X~1,…X~P)=(X1,…,XK)⋅GTtop+(Z1,…,ZT)⋅Gtopbot(\tilde{X}_1,\ldots \tilde{X}_P)=(X_1,\ldots,X_K)\cdot G^{top}_\mathcal{T}+(Z_1,\ldots,Z_T)\cdot G^{bot}_\mathcal{top}(X~1​,…X~P​)=(X1​,…,XK​)⋅GTtop​+(Z1​,…,ZT​)⋅Gtopbot​.

  • GTtopG^{top}_\mathcal{T}GTtop​, GTbotG^{bot}_\mathcal{T}GTbot​ contain the first TTT columns of GtopG^{top}Gtop, GbotG^{bot}Gbot, respectively.

Note that Gtop∈FqT×PG^{top}\in \mathbb{F}^{T\times P}_qGtop∈FqT×P​ is MDS, and hence GTtopG^{top}_\mathcal{T}GTtop​ is a T×TT\times TT×T invertible matrix.

Since Z=(Z1,…,ZT)Z=(Z_1,\ldots,Z_T)Z=(Z1​,…,ZT​) chosen uniformly random, so Z⋅GTbotZ\cdot G^{bot}_\mathcal{T}Z⋅GTbot​ is a one-time pad.

Same proof for decoding, we only need K+1K+1K+1 item to make the interpolation work.

Conclusion

  • Theorem: Lagrange Coded Computing is resilient against SSS stragglers, AAA adversaries, and TTT colluding nodes if P≥(K+T−1)deg⁡f+S+2A+1P\geq (K+T-1)\deg f+S+2A+1P≥(K+T−1)degf+S+2A+1
    • Privacy (increase with deg⁡f\deg fdegf) cost more than the straggler and adversary (increase linearly).
  • Caveat: Requires finite field arithmetic!
  • Some follow-up works analyzed information leakage over the reals

Side note for Blockchain

Blockchain: A decentralized system for trust management.

Blockchain maintains a chain of blocks.

  • A block contains a set of transactions.
  • Transaction = value transfer between clients.
  • The chain is replicated on each node.

Periodically, a new block is proposed and appended to each local chain.

  • The block must not contain invalid transactions.
  • Nodes must agree on proposed block.

Existing systems:

  • All nodes perform the same set of tasks.
  • Every node must receive every block.

Performance does not scale with number of node

Improving performance of blockchain

The performance of blockchain is inherently limited by its design.

  • All nodes perform the same set of tasks.
  • Every node must receive every block.

Idea: Combine blockchain with distributed computing.

  • Node tasks should complement each other.

Sharding (notion from databases):

  • Nodes are partitioned into groups of equal size.
  • Each group maintains a local chain.
  • More nodes, more groups, more transactions can be processed.
  • Better performance.

Security Problem

Biggest problem in blockchains: Adversarial (Byzantine) nodes.

  • Malicious actors wish to include invalid transactions.

Solution in traditional blockchains: Consensus mechanisms.

  • Algorithms for decentralized agreement.
  • Tolerates up to 1/31/31/3 Byzantine nodes.

Problem: Consensus conflicts with sharding.

  • Traditional consensus mechanisms tolerate ≈1/3\approx 1/3≈1/3 Byzantine nodes.
  • If we partition PPP nodes into KKK groups, we can tolerate only P/3KP/3KP/3K node failures.
    • Down from P/3P/3P/3 in non-shared systems.

Goal: Solve the consensus problem in sharded systems.

Tool: Coded computing.

Problem formulation

At epoch ttt of a shared blockchain system, we have

  • KKK local chain Y1t−1,…,YKt−1Y_1^{t-1},\ldots, Y_K^{t-1}Y1t−1​,…,YKt−1​.
  • KKK new blocks X1(t),…,XK(t)X_1(t),\ldots,X_K(t)X1​(t),…,XK​(t).
  • A polynomial verification function f(Xk(t),Ykt)f(X_k(t),Y_k^t)f(Xk​(t),Ykt​), which validates Xk(t)X_k(t)Xk​(t).

Proof

Balance check function f(Xk(t),Ykt)=∑τYk(τ)−Xk(t)f(X_k(t),Y_k^t)=\sum_\tau Y_k(\tau)-X_k(t)f(Xk​(t),Ykt​)=∑τ​Yk​(τ)−Xk​(t).

More commonly, a (polynomial) hash function. Used to:

  • Verify the sender’s public key.
  • Verify the ownership of the transferred funds.

Need: Apply a polynomial functions on KKK datasets.

Lagrange coded computing!

Blockchain with Lagrange coded computing

At epoch ttt:

  • A leader is elected (using secure election mechanism).
  • The leader receives new blocks X1(t),…,XK(t)X_1(t),\ldots,X_K(t)X1​(t),…,XK​(t).
  • The leader disperses the encoded blocks X~1(t),…,X~P(t)\tilde{X}_1(t),\ldots,\tilde{X}_P(t)X~1​(t),…,X~P​(t) to nodes.
    • Needs secure information dispersal mechanisms.

Every node i∈[P]i\in [P]i∈[P]:

  • Locally stores a coded chain Y~it\tilde{Y}_i^tY~it​ (encoded using LCC).
  • Receives X~i(t)\tilde{X}_i(t)X~i​(t).
  • Computes f(X~i(t),Y~it)f(\tilde{X}_i(t),\tilde{Y}_i^t)f(X~i​(t),Y~it​) and sends to the leader.

The leader decodes to get {f(Xi(t),Yit)}i=1K\{f(X_i(t),Y_i^t)\}_{i=1}^K{f(Xi​(t),Yit​)}i=1K​ and disperse securely to nodes.

Node iii appends coded block X~i(t)\tilde{X}_i(t)X~i​(t) to coded chain Y~it\tilde{Y}_i^tY~it​ (zeroing invalid transactions).

Guarantees security if P≥(K+T−1)deg⁡f+S+2A+1P\geq (K+T-1)\deg f+S+2A+1P≥(K+T−1)degf+S+2A+1.

  • AAA adversaries, degree ddd verification polynomial.

Sharding without sharding:

  • Computations are done on (coded) partial chains/blocks.
    • Good performance!
  • Since blocks/chains are coded, they are “dispersed” among many nodes.
    • Security problem in sharding solved!
  • Since the encoding is done (securely) through a leader, no need to send every block to all nodes.
    • Reduced communication! (main bottleneck).

Novelties:

  • First decentralized verification system with less than size of blocks times the number of nodes communication.
  • Coded consensus – Reach consensus on coded data.
Last updated on April 16, 2026
